questions.question: Define a 2-4 tree and state its fundamental properties.
keyPoints: 2-4 tree can have 2, 3, or 4 children per node
Contains 1, 2, or 3 keys respectively
All leaf nodes are at the same level (balanced)
Also known as 2-3-4 tree

questions.question: What is the minimum degree 't' in a B-Tree? Explain the constraints on the number of keys in a B-Tree node.
keyPoints: t is the minimum degree that determines node capacity
Minimum keys in non-root node: t-1
Maximum keys in any node: 2t-1
Minimum children in non-root node: ⌈m/2⌉ = t

questions.question: Explain the relationship between B-Tree order (m) and minimum degree (t).
keyPoints: Order m = maximum number of children per node
Minimum degree t = ⌈m/2⌉
Maximum keys = m - 1 = 2t - 1
Example: B-Tree of order 5 has t=3, max 5 keys per node

questions.question: Describe the procedure for inserting a key into a B-Tree.
keyPoints: 1) Search for appropriate leaf position
2) If leaf not full, insert key in sorted order
3) If leaf full, split the node
4) Middle key moves to parent
5) Repeat splitting up the tree if needed

questions.question: Why are B-Trees preferred over binary search trees for database indexing?
keyPoints: High branching factor reduces tree height
Fewer disk I/O operations needed
All keys stored at all levels (not just leaves)
Guaranteed O(log n) time complexity
Optimal for systems with large amounts of data

questions.question: Differentiate between a 2-node, 3-node, and 4-node in a 2-4 tree.
keyPoints: 2-node: 1 key, 2 children (like binary tree node)
3-node: 2 keys, 3 children
4-node: 3 keys, 4 children
All nodes maintain keys in sorted order

questions.question: What is node splitting in a B-Tree? When does it occur?
keyPoints: Splitting occurs when a node becomes full
A full node has 2t-1 keys
Split into two nodes with t-1 keys each
Middle key (tth key) moves up to parent
Maintains B-Tree balance property

questions.question: Define a B-Tree of order m. State and prove the properties of B-Trees. Also derive the relationship between height and number of keys.
keyPoints: Definition: B-Tree of order m satisfies: (i) Every node has at most m children (ii) Non-root has at least ⌈m/2⌉ children (iii) All leaves at same level (iv) Non-leaf with k children has k-1 keys
Height h ≥ 1: minimum keys = 2(⌈m/2⌉)^(h-1) - 1
Maximum keys = m^h - 1
Proof uses recursive property of minimum branching
Logarithmic height ensures O(log n) operations

questions.question: Explain the search operation in a B-Tree with a suitable algorithm. Analyze its time complexity.
keyPoints: Algorithm: (1) Start at root (2) Compare key with keys in current node using binary search (3) If found, return (4) If not, determine appropriate child subtree (5) Repeat until leaf or found
Time Complexity: O(logₜ n) where t is minimum degree
At each level, binary search takes O(log t) = O(1)
Total: O(t) × O(logₜ n) = O(log n)
Disk I/O: O(logₜ n) pages accessed

questions.question: Explain the relationship between Red-Black trees and 2-4 trees. How does a Red-Black tree represent a 2-4 tree?
keyPoints: Red-Black trees are binary tree representations of 2-4 trees
Black nodes represent 2-4 tree nodes
Red nodes represent children merged with parent 4-nodes
Transformation rules: (i) 2-node → black node (ii) 3-node → black parent + red child (iii) 4-node → black parent + 2 red children
Red-Black tree height ≤ 2 × log₂ n (equivalent to 2-4 tree balance)
Both provide O(log n) operations

questions.question: Describe the deletion operation in a B-Tree. Explain the cases that need to be handled.
keyPoints: Case 1: Key in leaf node - simply delete if node has > t-1 keys
Case 2: Key in internal node - replace with predecessor or successor, then delete from leaf
Case 3: Leaf has only t-1 keys - borrow from sibling or merge with sibling
Borrowing: redistribute keys between sibling and parent
Merging: combine two t-1 nodes with parent key → one node with 2t-2 keys
If parent becomes deficient, propagate fix up the tree
